author: @Nick.Baigent
text: Presumably with this arrangement the cogs are more prone to wear as they are constantly re-aligning and rubbing. Probably needs greasing for long term use

replies.author: @martinbonfiore7871
replies.text: I suspect you are correct about the wear issue.  Certainly other high reduction gear geometries like worm gears have a  rubbing issue and therefore benefit from grease.  I wonder is that any gear geometry that provides very high gear ratios with a few gears results in the rubbing and the need for grease...?   Always the trade offs....

author: @VeryWarmBear1
text:
The problem with 3D print pouts, as you have found, is the bond between the layers is
mechanically week. I think if you coat the printed part with a release agent pour plaster
of paris to incase it, let dry for 2 days, till dry and solid. Put the mold in an oven
slowly bring up the temp to the melting temp of the filament used  let bake at that temp
for an hour. remove carefully and let it completely cool. Break it gently out of the plaster.
After clean up the result should similar to an injection molded part. Time consuming but
useful as a one use injection mold.

author: @ToddHoldaway
text: Dear sir ..

author: @martinbonfiore7871
text: Fascinating!  My hobby is astrophotography and making telescope mounts.  Classically, these mounts use worm gears to achieve the high reductions needed (the telescope axis needs to move 15 degrees per hour to compensate for the earth's rotation).  Backlash in the gears is one of the fundamental limits to good tracking so the best mounts use very expensive worm gear sets.  I have never seen anyone use a perpetual wedge in this application and wonder if it might makes senses.  Also, I have searched the web for more info on the perpetual wedge and can find precious little.  It is a mystery to me why it appears to be so obscure and yet so potentially useful....

replies.author: @dragoscoco2173
replies.text: The backlash is enormous on these, so precision is out of the question. It can be mitigated by some torque spring to always force the system in one direction though, this would be recommended in any setup, but implies complexity and another failure point.

author: @peterstanford9927
text: If I understand the arrangements, at points of the revolution of the little planitary spur gear around the eccentric top gear, it must, of necessity not be fully meshed for some of it's motion. I am guessung the likes of Henry Royce would not have approved, if that were the case, and loading of the system must be compromised...or have I misunderstood?

author: @ImmortanJoeCamel
text: Hang on. Wouldn't the ring gear one in the configuration shown with the three gears have binding issues? If there's only one tooth difference and there are three gears arranged around the central axis evenly, they will be trying to force their own alignment. Because the gears are kind of free floating I suppose it would be a misalignment by roughly 1/6 of a tooth if divided evenly.

text:
There must be a huge amount of wear?
How does it compare to a worm gear of the same ratio?

replies.author: @Frisenette
replies.text: They are quite common. But of course there is a lot of friction too and an oil film will get penetrated easily with the forces involved.

replies.author: @dragoscoco2173
replies.text: Wear is not really an issue for such low grearing with minimal friction, lubed of course. It does have loads of backlash if spun in the other direction, so not great for precision were worm gears of any ratio needed do the job just fine, but worm gears have quite high friction.

author: @ewjorgy
text:
Hi Robert,

That is a gear version of the age old Spanish windlass.  The Spanish windlass has a rope that winds around a shaft with a certain diameter, the rope goes down to a load hanging on a pulley (usually a bucket in a well) and the rope comes back up to the same shaft but with a slightly smaller diameter.  The difference in diameter of the shaft sections determines the "gear ratio" of the system.  I have never seen a gear version though!   Sooooo cool!  Thank you!

All the best from California! 🧡

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - i didn't know that

replies.author: @klausnielsen1537
replies.text: Hi there. I thought the Spanish windlass was used for tightening rope by twisting it with a stick. But I can imagine how effective the service you describe would be for heavy loads.

author: @ΜικεΡ
text:
This is sort of similar to a strain-wave gear aka harmonic drive (I think Harmonic Drive is a company who makes these types of actuators)

Its a similar idea, you have a stator and rotor with a 1-tooth difference, but instead of a planetary gear you have a flexible gear thats pushed into mesh by a cam (wave generator)

I tried making one a few years ago for use with a BLDC motor. I used a timing belt for the flexible gear and 3D printed the rotor, stator and wave generator. The mechanism worked, but there was too much friction to be useful.

replies.author: @86larsonrd
replies.text: @CraigLandsberg-lk1ep  it give you a 100 to ratio of torque.  He didn't show how it attaches, but remember the dot?  If that was attached to a rope, it would pull at 100 times the force (roughly....) that is used to turn the crank.

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
It's not 1% difference, the output is 1% of the rotationspeed of the input.
Which in theory results in 100x the torque.
(In reality adding a gearbox adds friction to your system, so...)
